findings.description: Row 3 has 3 column(s); the header row and earlier data rows have 2. Different parsers resolve the mismatch differently: pandas pads with NaN, awk truncates, Excel carries the mismatch through as a ragged row, and Python's csv module returns the row verbatim. The divergence is the concealment surface.
findings.concealed: expected 2, got 3: parser disagreement. Surplus cell content (1 extra cell(s)): 'HIDDEN_TEXT_PAYLOAD: actual revenue $10,000 - see annex'

findings.description: JSON object contains key '__proto__' at $.__proto__, a canonical JavaScript prototype-pollution name. Recursive-merge consumers (Lodash _.merge, jQuery $.extend, minimist, dozens of others) treat such a key as a prototype-chain mutation primitive rather than data. A human reader who sees this as JSON data does not see the hazard.
findings.surface: object key '__proto__' (treated as data by the human reader)
findings.concealed: sibling keys in the same object: ['description', 'constructor']

findings.description: JSON object contains key 'constructor' at $.constructor, a canonical JavaScript prototype-pollution name. Recursive-merge consumers (Lodash _.merge, jQuery $.extend, minimist, dozens of others) treat such a key as a prototype-chain mutation primitive rather than data. A human reader who sees this as JSON data does not see the hazard.
findings.surface: object key 'constructor' (treated as data by the human reader)
findings.concealed: sibling keys in the same object: ['description', '__proto__']

findings.description: JSON object contains key 'prototype' at $.constructor.prototype, a canonical JavaScript prototype-pollution name. Recursive-merge consumers (Lodash _.merge, jQuery $.extend, minimist, dozens of others) treat such a key as a prototype-chain mutation primitive rather than data. A human reader who sees this as JSON data does not see the hazard.
findings.surface: object key 'prototype' (treated as data by the human reader)
findings.concealed: sibling keys in the same object: []

findings.description: 1 zero-width character(s) in this cell value (U+200B) — invisible to a human reader, preserved by parsers and tokenizers.
findings.concealed: 1 zero-width codepoint(s)

findings.description: 1 bidi-control character(s) in this cell value (U+202E) — reorders display without changing the codepoint stream.
findings.concealed: 1 bidi-override codepoint(s)

findings.description: Cell at row 2, column 0 ('description') carries 1 Unicode bidi control codepoint(s): ['U+202E']. The bidi algorithm reorders the visual rendering of the surrounding text in spreadsheet renderers (Excel, LibreOffice Calc, Google Sheets); the on-screen glyph order can differ from the byte order. There is no legitimate use of bidi override codepoints inside a CSV data cell.
findings.concealed: second​‮ invoice

findings.description: Cell at row 2, column 0 ('description') carries 1 zero-width codepoint(s): ['U+200B']. Zero-width characters render as no glyph in every viewer (spreadsheet, text editor, browser). The cell looks clean; the bytes carry the payload. There is no legitimate use of zero-width codepoints inside a CSV data cell.
findings.concealed: second​‮ invoice

findings.description: Cell at row 2, column 0 decodes to different strings under UTF-8 and latin-1. The byte stream carries an encoding-fork payload: a UTF-8 reader and a latin-1 reader will see different cell text from the same bytes. UTF-8 decode: 'second\u200b\u202e invoice'; latin-1 decode: 'secondâ\x80\x8bâ\x80® invoice'.
findings.concealed: UTF-8: 'second\u200b\u202e invoice'; latin-1: 'secondâ\x80\x8bâ\x80® invoice'

findings.description: Row 3 has an odd number of unescaped double-quote characters (1 after ``""`` pairs removed). Different parsers resolve the unbalanced quote differently — some consume the next row as continuation of the quoted field, some raise, some truncate at the quote. The divergence is a classic tier-3 concealment shape.
findings.concealed: odd quote count — parser disagreement on field boundaries

findings.description: Row 5 has an odd number of unescaped double-quote characters (3 after ``""`` pairs removed). Different parsers resolve the unbalanced quote differently — some consume the next row as continuation of the quoted field, some raise, some truncate at the quote. The divergence is a classic tier-3 concealment shape.
findings.concealed: odd quote count — parser disagreement on field boundaries

findings.description: CSV file begins with a UTF-8 BOM (EF BB BF). Most modern readers strip it, but naive parsers (awk, Python's ``open(..., newline='')`` without explicit ``utf-8-sig`` encoding, some shell tools) carry it into the first header cell — the cell named ``Date`` in a human view reads as ``\ufeffDate`` to a downstream filter. The asymmetry is the concealment shape.
findings.surface: (header row appears normal in BOM-aware readers)
findings.concealed: leading BOM may be preserved by naive readers

findings.description: CSV bytes are not valid UTF-8 — decode failed at offset 326 (byte 0xE9). Bytes are valid Latin-1, so a UTF-8-aware reader (pandas default, Python 3 ``open``) will see a UnicodeDecodeError while a Latin-1-aware reader (Excel's default on Windows, legacy shell tools) sees renderable but different glyphs. The asymmetry is the concealment shape.
findings.surface: (renders as Latin-1 glyphs in some readers)
findings.concealed: bytes decode to different glyphs under UTF-8 vs. Latin-1

findings.description: Cell at row 5, column 2 decodes to different strings under UTF-8 and latin-1. The byte stream carries an encoding-fork payload: a UTF-8 reader and a latin-1 reader will see different cell text from the same bytes. UTF-8 decode: 'caf� receipt'; latin-1 decode: 'café receipt'.
findings.concealed: UTF-8: 'caf� receipt'; latin-1: 'café receipt'

findings.description: Duplicate key 'amount_usd' in a JSON object (count=2). Different parsers may resolve duplicates differently. The document presents different meanings to different readers; the first and last occurrence values are surfaced below.
findings.surface: key 'amount_usd' (later value wins in most parsers)
findings.concealed: first occurrence: 1000; last occurrence (parser wins): 'HIDDEN_TEXT_PAYLOAD: actual revenue $10,000 - see annex'

findings.description: could not parse JSON: Expecting value at line 1 column 1
findings.concealed: (absence of findings is not evidence of cleanness)

findings.description: JSON line comment found at line 1 column 1. RFC 8259 (strict JSON) does not permit comments; lenient parsers (JSON5, jsonc) silently ignore them. The comment text is invisible to any post-parse tree walk: the byte stream carries the payload, the parsed object never sees it.
findings.surface: line comment (invisible to JSON tree walk)
findings.concealed: line comment text: ' HIDDEN_TEXT_PAYLOAD: actual revenue $10,000 - see annex'

findings.description: JSON block comment found at line 4 column 3. RFC 8259 (strict JSON) does not permit comments; lenient parsers (JSON5, jsonc) silently ignore them. The comment text is invisible to any post-parse tree walk: the byte stream carries the payload, the parsed object never sees it.
findings.surface: block comment (invisible to JSON tree walk)
findings.concealed: block comment text: ' comment block: HIDDEN_TEXT_PAYLOAD: actual revenue $10,000 - see annex '

findings.description: could not parse JSON: Extra data at line 87 column 1
findings.concealed: (absence of findings is not evidence of cleanness)

findings.description: JSON document carries non-whitespace content after the root value, starting at line 87 column 1 (byte offset 3949). Strict-JSON parsers reject this outright; lenient consumers (raw_decode, jq, streaming JSON, naive ``JSON.parse`` after a slice) silently discard the suffix. The trailing bytes are invisible to any tool that walks the parsed value alone.
findings.surface: trailing content past root close (strict-JSON violation)
findings.concealed: trailing bytes preview: 'HIDDEN_TEXT_PAYLOAD: actual revenue $10,000 - see annex\n'
